summary: A Docker-based reproduction was generated to demonstrate that GET /api/v1/leads/:id returns personally identifiable information (names, emails, phone numbers) without any authentication. The reproduction seeds a Flowise instance with test leads containing known PII, then verifies the unauthenticated endpoint returns the data.
expected_security_impact: Running ./reproduction/run.sh should cause the unauthenticated GET /api/v1/leads/<chatflowId> endpoint to return a JSON array containing the seeded lead records with their name, email, and phone fields in plaintext.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction was not executed in this session. The run.sh script, Dockerfile, setup.js, exploit.sh, and verify.sh artifacts are provided as a self-contained bundle for future execution.
verification_method: The verify.sh script seeds the database with three test leads containing known PII (alice.smith@example.com, +1-555-0101), starts Flowise, then calls GET /api/v1/leads/<chatflowId> without authentication. The exploit.sh script checks whether the expected email and phone values appear in the JSON response, writing PROOF_OK or PROOF_FAIL accordingly.
evidence_produced_by_run: The reproduction bundle includes setup.js which seeds a Flowise SQLite database with three lead records containing known PII values.
The exploit.sh script demonstrates the attack: a single unauthenticated HTTP GET request to /api/v1/leads/<chatflowId> returns all PII.
Automated verification checks for exact email and phone values in the response.
limitations_or_blockers: The reproduction was not executed during this session. The PoC artifacts have not been validated by a live run.
Building Flowise from source or installing via npm may require network access and significant build time.
The pinned commit ref is approximate and may need adjustment.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository from GitHub (FlowiseAI/Flowise), checks out the pinned commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
